The employment component stood out as notably strong.

Saylor could've been the countercyclical force for BTC: 

sell some into pumps, buy back the dips.

Sure, bull runs might end lower but bottoms also shallower.

Instead his structure amplifies volatility both ways. 

The NAV premium only lets him buy when BTC pumps, and fixed dividends force him to sell the lows.

Volatility is great for SOME traders. 

But BTC's pitch to institutions is that it calms down as it matures.

The biggest holder is making it worse.

* LLMs giving simple explanations & visualizations on demand for anything I don't understand

* Getting prompted in the evenings for daily micro-lessons on Telegram by my Hermes Agent, which keeps track of my learning & lesson plans

The educational renaissance is here. Just not evenly distributed.

We now believe the S&P 500 is setting up for 8,000+. Here's why:

Chip stocks have quietly become the new leaders of this bull market.

While many of the Magnificent 7 stocks have declined 20%+ from their recent highs, semiconductor names have taken over.

In fact, 8 of the 10 best-performing S&P 500 stocks this year are in the chip industry.

As a result, the S&P 500 now sits just ~1% below a record high despite weakness the big tech stocks which have led the market since 2022.

This marks the first time since 2022 that the market traded highly with leadership outside of the Magnificent 7, while the Magnificent 7 traded in the opposite direction.

In our view, this rotation is constructive as the Magnificent 7 prepares to "sub back in."

As large-cap tech begins to regain leadership while semiconductors continue to outperform or even cool-off, the S&P 500 is poised for 8,000+.

Asset owners will continue to win.

"Less well understood is that for decades, China has been creating money, equivalent to the US M2, at a scale much in excess of its domestic economy’s growth needs. tweets.text: On this day in 1785, the Continental Congress unanimously voted to adopt the dollar as the official monetary unit of the United States, replacing the chaotic mix of British pounds, Spanish reales, and state-issued paper that had circulated since independence. Thomas Jefferson proposed the decimal system, arguing that dividing a dollar into 100 cents was far simpler than the British system of pounds, shillings, and pence. The name "dollar" came from the Spanish silver dollar (itself derived from the German "thaler"), which was already the most widely circulated coin in the colonies. Congress wouldn't actually mint its own coins until the Coinage Act of 1792 established the U.S. Mint in Philadelphia. The decision to go decimal was so practical that it influenced monetary reform worldwide: France adopted a decimal currency in 1795, and most of the world eventually followed.

#AI needs power.

A lot of it.

And the buildout to feed that demand runs through #nuclear (#uranium), energy storage, grid expansion and semiconductors.

There is one surprisingly overlooked link between them:

#Fluorspar.

Fluorspar is the only commercial source of fluorine. Uranium enrichment depends on UF₆. EV batteries can require 5–10x more fluorspar than lithium. Advanced chip manufacturing relies on fluorine chemistry.

Yet the U.S. produces zero primary fluorspar.

It disagreed with the timeline.

Some bearish takes on the hottest stablecoin launch, and bullish takes on sectors everyone abandoned.

8 reads that will sharpen your thinking:

Corporate DAO of 150 rivals

OUSD was the biggest announcement yesterday.

Most people were bullish on the latest stablecoin. $CRCL fell ~13%. But big names don't mean actual adoption. Here's a good contrarian take:

Preparation for Next Cycle.

This bear is fundamentally different.

Nobody reads whitepapers anymore. Projects don't write them either. But now we have killer apps (perps, PMs) & revenue generators.

Here's Ignas's take:

Mental Model for Compute Markets

Many are hyping it up. According to Tarun, it's very early. Like early fracking and pre-ETF BTC.

The underlying asset is clearly real and in demand, but market structure, pricing, and hedging instruments are still immature and inefficient.

Decentralized Inference Deep Dive

It's a category where crypto x AI is already producing real value.

This article dives into the challenges of the sector, leading projects, investment filters, and more.

Airdrop opportunities

Nobody seems to be farming them anymore.

But if you're still onchain, there are many solid tokenless projects. This listicle puts such Solana projects into a tier-list based on airdrop potential.

A $SOL Bull Case

You will trade everything onchain

Tokenized equities, bridged majors, exotic RWAs, all settling on one chain. Anders lays out why Solana's stack (Firedancer, Alpenglow's 150ms finality, propAMMs) makes it the venue.

The SoV Race

Store-of-value is a multi-trillion opportunity.

BTC, ETH, and ZEC are the leading contenders among crypto assets. Each wins a different axis: BTC on memetics, ETH on durability, ZEC on censorship resistance.

tweets.text:
tldr even if ethereum becomes giga successful -- priority fees will probably not rise and may even go down a lot, and that is a good thing.

if ethereum scales in both tech and adoption, base fees (i.e. the fixed fee paid by the marginal transaction for inclusion in the block) can certainly increase a lot, and there isn't really a ceiling for that. 

1M txns/s paid $0.1 cent each? that's $315b in run rate revenue per year. something like that is probably Ethereum's bull case for me.

but there is simply no such case for prio fees to increase a lot, and one big reason to believe they will actually continue to fall over time.

for a primer, searchers and retail traders pay prio fees  price to access a *specific* piece of state before other transactors. a block can have a base fee of 1 gwei, with someone paying 1m gwei to access the first slot in the block. even though ethereum's adoption and txn activity increased, prio fees have stayed relatively constant at 500-600m/year for the last 4 years. 

many mev experts, including me, have predicted that priority fees would likely decrease even as activity increases a lot, and the reason is that most of these conflicts should never "trickle down" to the validator to begin with. they are like free dollar bills that someone drops on the street, and if they are smart, they will simply stop dropping it. and if they are not smart, someone who is will replace their app over time.

when you zoom in on what conflicts searchers are paying for priority in, its primarily two things: arbitrage and liquidations (which are itself a form of arbitrage of buying an underpriced lending market position and selling it for a profit). 

in both cases, the conflict used to reach the chain even though there sits someone upstream who could resolve this conflict already: e.g. a wallets running backrunning auctions on their user's orderflow (metamask, rabby, everyone), DEXs selling the first right to arbitrage (angstrom), DEXs paying to update their quotes before the first order can hit per block (propAMMs), lending markets selling the right to liquidate a position (chainlink svr + aave) - the list goes on. 

even though I predicted this trend of "apps taking control of their own ordering" for years, 2026 is the year when it finally reached not just wallets but DEXs and lending markets as well, at scale, and Defi is becoming much better for it. 

this has several consequences for ethereum: if you think that Defi is ethereum's main usecase and will be responsible for much of its future growth, this trend is amazing. it will make Defi spreads tighter and fees lower, making it ultimately more competitive against cefi and tradfi markets.

second, prio fees will likely never matter again for stakers, and should not be a big part of the discussion for issuance reduction. another update that comes up occasionally is mev-burn - on the face of it, probably not worth the complexity.

if you take away one thing from the thread, its that "95% of priority fees belong the apps or wallets who create them, and will flow there over time". ethereum has been benefiting from the inefficiency of the apps built on top of it for so much value to trickle down, but this trend is now in full reversal. and a future when apps make trillions of trading and lending volume per day, while paying hardly any priority fees - that future is actually very good, because it means the apps are well designed and competitive with tradfi, in spite of running on a blockchain. 

if Ethereum wins, it will be on the back of a small base fee that millions of actors pay, that can still accumulate to a whole lot of revenue per year.

tldr even if ethereum becomes giga successful -- priority fees will probably not rise and may even go down a lot, and that is a good thing.

if ethereum scales in both tech and adoption, base fees (i.e. the fixed fee paid by the marginal transaction for inclusion in the block) can certainly increase a lot, and there isn't really a ceiling for that. 

1M txns/s paid $0.1 cent each? that's $315b in run rate revenue per year. something like that is probably Ethereum's bull case for me.

but there is simply no such case for prio fees to increase a lot, and one big reason to believe they will actually continue to fall over time.

for a primer, searchers and retail traders pay prio fees  price to access a *specific* piece of state before other transactors. a block can have a base fee of 1 gwei, with someone paying 1m gwei to access the first slot in the block. even though ethereum's adoption and txn activity increased, prio fees have stayed relatively constant at 500-600m/year for the last 4 years. 

many mev experts, including me, have predicted that priority fees would likely decrease even as activity increases a lot, and the reason is that most of these conflicts should never "trickle down" to the validator to begin with. they are like free dollar bills that someone drops on the street, and if they are smart, they will simply stop dropping it. and if they are not smart, someone who is will replace their app over time.

when you zoom in on what conflicts searchers are paying for priority in, its primarily two things: arbitrage and liquidations (which are itself a form of arbitrage of buying an underpriced lending market position and selling it for a profit). 

in both cases, the conflict used to reach the chain even though there sits someone upstream who could resolve this conflict already: e.g. a wallets running backrunning auctions on their user's orderflow (metamask, rabby, everyone), DEXs selling the first right to arbitrage (angstrom), DEXs paying to update their quotes before the first order can hit per block (propAMMs), lending markets selling the right to liquidate a position (chainlink svr + aave) - the list goes on. 

even though I predicted this trend of "apps taking control of their own ordering" for years, 2026 is the year when it finally reached not just wallets but DEXs and lending markets as well, at scale, and Defi is becoming much better for it. 

this has several consequences for ethereum: if you think that Defi is ethereum's main usecase and will be responsible for much of its future growth, this trend is amazing. it will make Defi spreads tighter and fees lower, making it ultimately more competitive against cefi and tradfi markets.

second, prio fees will likely never matter again for stakers, and should not be a big part of the discussion for issuance reduction. another update that comes up occasionally is mev-burn - on the face of it, probably not worth the complexity.

if you take away one thing from the thread, its that "95% of priority fees belong the apps or wallets who create them, and will flow there over time". ethereum has been benefiting from the inefficiency of the apps built on top of it for so much value to trickle down, but this trend is now in full reversal. and a future when apps make trillions of trading and lending volume per day, while paying hardly any priority fees - that future is actually very good, because it means the apps are well designed and competitive with tradfi, in spite of running on a blockchain. 

if Ethereum wins, it will be on the back of a small base fee that millions of actors pay, that can still accumulate to a whole lot of revenue per year.

@potuz_eth are you talking about any specific mev burn proposal?

I think it remains to be seen how it will fare compared to more traditional seat-based colocation models. definitely kudos to them for experimenting

but importantly, it validates what I wrote above - apps will eventually internalize, regulate, and monetize every piece of financial/info flow of their apps. 

hyperliquid just has a chain attached to it, but these prio fees are at the app level, and I never expect prio fees on hyperevm proper to grow anywhere near Ethereum levels

I did not always believe in the four year cycle for Bitcoin.

I used to think it was silly, and that the market had to be more complicated than that.

But then the market proved me wrong. So I admitted I was wrong, learned from my mistakes, and became a better investor.

This cycle, a lot of new Bitcoiners faded the four year cycle and relentlessly mocked anyone calling for the bear market. For many of them, instead of recognizing they were wrong, they continue to laugh at anyone willing to discuss the simplicity of the four year cycle.

They are likely doomed to repeat the same mistakes in the future, since their ego gets in the way of them being able to admit that they were wrong.

tweets.text:
After some thought, I still don't agree with the red card call on Balogun, as the other player ran into him from behind which arguably caused him to stumble in an awkward way. I'm sure many will disagree with that as the still image looks pretty bad. But if you look at how the play developed, I think it provides some missing context. I think Balogun deserved a yellow card, but not a red.

With that said, two wrongs do not make a right. I'm not thrilled with the US president getting involved in the matter. The truth is that if this were any other team, I would not be happy about the unfair treatment. Yes, Ronaldo was able to play in the world cup after a similar ruling, but in this case the ruling occurred after the US president called FIFA, which optically is bad.

I wish that red cards did not cause the player to miss future games against a team that was not even involved with the red card, but that is how the rules are written. And if other teams have to abide by those rules, then so too should the USA.

I will be pulling for the USA tonight regardless of whether Balogun plays or not, but I think it would be good for the integrity of the game for presidents to not contact FIFA to inquire about referee decisions.

I lived in Quebec for eight years ... I love it. Everything about Quebec, but especially l'hiver. 

One thing I learned about Quebec is that it is quite wonderfully parochial. It just does not care much about what the rest of the world is doing. Its always going to be dancing to its own tune, and that's great. I salute that. In today's world, ignorance really is bliss. 

The problem is that we no longer live in 1995, and I am not sure that sovereignists in Quebec get that. Back in the heyday of America's magnanimous hegemony and globalization, it made sense for Quebec to become independent and simply melt into the world (as say, Slovenia did). Today, with the US openly invoking territorial expansion in North America,  the moment has passed. 

It was always adorable to me that my Quebecois friends would use the example of "what happened to the francophones of Louisiana" as the reason to secede from Canada. When it is precisely the reason to stay in Canada. 

What's more likely? Quebec to lose its identity within Canada (we kind of have 300 years of evidence that it will not!) or within a North America whose borders are changing? 

Let me be blunt to all the sovereignists out there: there is zero chance that this America lets you have your francophone/socialist paradise alongside St. Lawrence in a multipolar world. Zero. Not gonna happen. Non. 

The future for Quebec is to redefine Canadian identity. To usurp it. It's Canadian to be francophone. Here, let me help: 

Quebec: Canada depuis 1534

https://t.co/SuYo11iu8O

tweets.text:
New blog post focused on a fun paper by @danivilardell77 and @sjbreck

They study voter privacy via bribery games and call it B-Privacy.     

Why?     

Privacy in weighted voting is harder than it looks.  

Even if every ballot is encrypted, the final tally itself can leak how people voted.    

Worse: that leakage can make vote buying enfoceable   

It is not straight forward to make a weighted voting system private without hiding the tally.    

So, can you do something in between, where there is "enough" voting privacy such that the briber's cost is increased by several multiples?     

Find out in the next tweet 👇
